conf-proc-title-group.conf-full-title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW)
conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW), 2025 IEEE International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW)

title-group.article-title: Semisl-Rdc: Semi-Supervised Rating Distribution Calibration Via Pivot User for Debiasing Recommendation Systems

conf-article-meta.aff:
University of Science and Technology of China \\ Hefei
, China \\ xiaxinyuan@mail.ustc.edu.cn

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-8132-9 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: Recommender systems are seen as an effective tool to address information overload, but the ratings they learn from are typically missing-not-at-random (MNAR), which undermines predictive fidelity. Existing debiasing approaches face three major limitations: (i) highly sensitive to imputation and propensity model misspecification; (ii) ignoring heterogeneous degrees of user-level selection bias, missing the opportunity to leverage missing-at-random (MAR) like pivot users as additional references; (iii) distribution-calibration strategies in self-supervised frameworks implicitly assume sufficient unbiased and observed feedback per user. We propose SemiSL-RDC, a semi-supervised expansion of rating distribution calibration. First, we identify pivot users, whose rating is closer to missing-at-random via a multi-criteria score that fuses mean absolute error on labeled pairs, normalized rating-distribution entropy, and labeled coverage. Second, we calibrate non-pivots by matching their predicted distributions to similar pivots with a lightweight user-level loss added to the base objective, using predictions over all items to form differentiable distribution features. Empirical experiments on 2 real-world datasets show improved effectiveness under scarce unbiased supervision, suggesting that semi-supervised pivot discovery plus distribution calibration offers a practical path to mitigating selection bias.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Recommendation systems have become ubiquitous tools to guide users through vast catalogs of content and products, yet the data they learn from are typically missing-not-at-random (MNAR) because users self-select what to rate [1]–[6]. Recent work further reveals that MNAR bias can stem from correlated latent exogenous variables ignored by standard models [7], or from extraneous sampling bias under complex missingness mechanisms [8], highlighting the need for more expressive causal formulations. Such biases distort both learning and offline evaluation, leading to poor generalization on unobserved data, and reliable accuracy ideally requires missing-at-random (MAR) feedback [9]–[13]. In practice, MAR supervision is scarce and unevenly distributed across users. For example, Yahoo! R3 [14], [15] contains 15,400 users and 1,000 items with 311,704 MNAR ratings plus a small $54,000-$ rating unbiased slice (10 random items for only 5,400 users), yielding an overall observation density of $\approx 2.37 \%$ and peruser MAR coverage ranging from 0 % to 1 % ($0 / 1000$ for non-MAR users; 10/1000 for MAR users). These statistics highlight three challenges we target: (1) unbiased feedback is small-scale and costly [16], [17]; (2) bias arises mainly from user self-selection rather than item logging; and (3) selection bias is heterogeneous across users [18], [19].

Prior debiasing approaches can be grouped into four categories. (1) Imputation/pseudo-labeling estimates missing ratings and trains on the completed matrix, but severe sparsity and unobserved user/item factors make accurate imputation difficult [20], [21]. (2) Propensity-based reweighting corrects exposure using estimated observation probabilities but is fragile to unobserved confounding and model misspecification [22], [23]. (3) Doubly-robust (DR) combines imputation and IPS to temper variance, yet bias-variance still depends on model specification [24]–[28]. Recent advances further refine DR into three complementary directions: (i) Bias-variance control, where CBPL unifies calibration and balancing losses to explicitly trade off bias and variance [29]; (ii) Variance-robust boosting, where DR-Boost introduces a doubly-robust ensemble to stabilize learning under limited supervision [30]; and (iii) Small-propensity stabilization, where Adaptive Structure Learning employs partial parameter sharing to prevent error amplification in low-coverage regions [31]. Meanwhile, other method like generative models seek to jointly model data generation and missingness, but face specification/identifiability risks in the presence of unobserved variables [32]–[34]. In parallel, reference-based balancing/calibration uses a cohort as "ground truth": BalIPS/DR learns model-agnostic balancing weights from a small, fixed unbiased set (effective but limited by size/coverage); Rating Distribution Calibration (RDC) aligns each user's predicted rating distribution to that of MAR-like peers without explicit propensity models, but estimates "pivotness" via a peruser self-learning task implicitly presumes enough unbiased feedbacks per user brittle under real-world sparsity [18], [19], [35]–[37].

Beyond these, causal inference is a commonly used techniques and has been proven useful in practice [38]–[42]. Recent bias-aware counterfactual and balancing paradigms have advanced the field. Entire-space counterfactual learning (ESCL) extends DR to model the full exposure space, improving reliability on sparse content [43], Optimal-transport causal estimation links recommendation debiasing to distribution alignment [44], and User-Distribution Mapping bridges crossdomain exposure gaps via collaborative filtering-based mapping [45]. From an industrial standpoint, Bayesian knowledge infusion shows promise for stabilizing inferential sensors and large-scale recommender pipelines [46]. Meanwhile, fairnessoriented and temporal-causal perspectives have emerged: networked interference correction ensures equitable influence propagation [47], and state-space counterfactual prediction leverages time-varying latent dynamics for sequential bias mitigation [48]. Complementary causal calibration strategies such as Rating Distribution Calibration (RDC) [19], BalIPS/DR, and CBPL [29] collectively form the foundation for modern debiasing frameworks in MNAR scenarios.

Motivated by these gaps, we propose Semi-Supervised Rating Distribution Calibration (SemiSL-RDC). We adopt the notion of pivot users-those whose feedback appears closer to MAR-and observe that users with weaker selection bias tend to admit more accurate predicted rating distributions, making them suitable references for calibrating others [18], [19], [49]. Rather than relying solely on scarce per-user unbiased labels or on a tiny, fixed unbiased set, we identify pivots via semi-supervised signals that combine labeled prediction error with unlabeled distributional evidence, then calibrate non-pivots toward similar pivots by matching differentiable distribution features computed from predictions over all items. This design explicitly addresses small MAR supervision and user-level heterogeneity while remaining model-agnostic. Our contributions are threefold:
Semi-supervised pivot discovery: we define a multicriteria pivot score that fuses per-user MAE only on labeled pairs, normalized rating-distribution entropy, and labeled coverage, followed by deterministic thresholding to obtain stable anchors under scarce unbiased supervision.
Pivot-aware calibration: we introduce a user-level calibration loss that aligns each non-pivot's predicted distribution to a soft neighborhood of similar pivots, augmenting the base objective without explicit propensity or DR modeling.
Empirical validation: on two real-world datasets, we observe consistent gains in ranking and distributional calibration under MNAR and label scarcity, compared with strong IPS/DR, residual/balancing, and prior RDC baselines.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Preliminaries
sec.p: In general, recommendation systems aim to predict the feedback (rating or preference) of a user to an item, and we adopt the user-item paired framework to formulate the MNAR issue formally. Let $\mathcal{U}=\left\{u_{1}, \ldots, u_{M}\right\}$ denotes users, $\mathcal{I}=\left\{i_{1}, \ldots, i_{N}\right\}$ denotes items, and $\mathcal{D}=\mathcal{U} \times \mathcal{I}$ denotes the set of all user-item pairs. For $(u, i) \in \mathcal{D}$, let $x_{u, i}$ denotes useritem features vector The binary rating for each user to an item is $y_{u, i}$, and we only able to observe those in an observed subset $\mathcal{O}=\left\{(u, i) \in \mathcal{D}: o_{u, i}=1\right\}$, where $o_{u, i} \in\{0,1\}$ indicates whether a label is observed.
where $\ell(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a standard pointwise loss, like cross entropy (CE), mean absolute error (MAE) or mean squared error (MSE).

If the missingness is missing at random, the naive estimator is unbiased to the ideal loss, but MAR-style evaluation slices are small and unevenly distributed across users. Following common experimental protocols and prior analyses, we assume the observed set is a mixture $\mathcal{O}=\mathcal{O}_{\text {MNAR }} \cup \mathcal{O}_{\text {MAR }}$ with $\left|\mathcal{O}_{\text {MNAR }}\right| \gg\left|\mathcal{O}_{\text {MAR }}\right|:$ a large MNAR portion collected from natural use, plus a small MAR-like portion (e.g., randomized unbiased slices) that is costly and uneven across users. Our methodology leverages this reality by identifying users whose behavior appears MAR-like and calibrating other users' predicted rating distributions toward those MAR-like references-details next.

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: Training on observational ratings alone overfits to missing-not-at-random (MNAR) exposure and yields poor generalization under MAR-style evaluation. We mitigate this through Semi-Supervised Rating Distribution Calibration (SemiSLRDC), designed around the same three real-world constraints articulated in the Introduction: (i) unbiased/MAR supervision is scarce and uneven across users; (ii) selection effects are largely driven by user self-selection (items are typically curated and registered by the platform); and (iii) users exhibit heterogeneous selection bias. Rather than relying on a tiny unbiased pool or fully specified propensity/imputation models, we leverage cross-user distributional guidance: a subset of users naturally behaves closer to MAR and therefore provides more reliable distributional targets for calibrating others. The challenge is to discover such users and use them without assuming abundant labels.

SemiSL-RDC operationalizes this idea with three integrated components trained in a single loop. First, we identify pivot users, those whose feedback patterns appear MAR-like, via a semi-supervised score that fuses labeled prediction error with unlabeled distributional evidence (normalized entropy and labeled-coverage), producing a calibrated probability later used to select a stable pivot set. Second, we construct differentiable user-level distribution features from predictions over all items (labeled and unlabeled) and define a pivot-aware neighborhood similarity that measures how a non-pivot's predicted rating distribution differs from those of similar pivots. Third, we add a calibration constraint to the base objective by augmenting the standard cross-entropy loss with a userlevel distribution-matching penalty; the combined loss jointly updates the predictor while shrinking non-pivot distributions toward MAR-like references. The next subsection details the semi-supervised pivot discovery, followed by the construction of distribution features and the calibration constraint.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Pivot User Identification based on a Semi-SL Task
sec.p: A pivot user is one whose feedback pattern is closer to MAR; intuitively, such users exhibit less extreme rating tendencies and more activity than heavily MNAR users, so their predicted rating distributions serve as more trustworthy targets for calibration. Prior self-supervised RDC estimated a per-user "unbiasedness" primarily from labeled accuracy (e.g., per-user MAE) and then randomly sampled pivots from those probabilities to avoid hard thresholds [18], [19]. However, with only a handful of unbiased labels per user, those label-only probabilities are high-variance and brittle. For example, a user who rates only positives while a misspecified predictor outputs positives for everything will display near-zero MAE on the few labeled pairs despite strong selection bias. This motivates a semi-supervised design that augments scarce labeled evidence with unlabeled distributional structure available at scale.

User-level criteria and pivot probability. For each user $u$, we compute three interpretable criteria that combine labeled and unlabeled signals and then map them to a pivot probability used for deterministic selection.
Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for labeled ratings. On the user's observed set $\mathcal{O}_{u}$,

This is the same label-only indicator textbfasized in the prior self-supervised RDC formulations. A smaller value suggests MAR-like behavior on the (few) labeled pairs, but alone can be misleading under severe sparsity.
Higher $H_{u}$ reflects a more balanced observed mix (not "all likes" or "all dislikes"), a proxy for weaker selection effects and a distribution closer to a MAR target [50], [51].
where $M$ is the number of items. Larger $C_{u}$ indicates more labeled coverage for $u$.

where $\sigma(t)=1 /(1+\exp (t))$ stands for the Sigmoid function, and $\pi_{u}$ is defined as the probability that user $u$ is a pivot user. We then select pivots deterministically by a fixed cutoff: users with $\pi_{u} \geq 0.5$ form pivot user set, $\mathcal{P}_{+}=\left\{u: \pi_{u} \geq 0.5\right\}$, and the remainder, non-pivot user set, form $\mathcal{P}_{-}$. A fixed threshold yields a stable anchor cohort while the semi-supervised score $\pi_{u}$ integrates both labeled accuracy and unlabeled distributional evidence, addressing the unreliability of MAE-only scoring under scarce MAR labels.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Semi-SL Rating Distribution Calibration
sec.p: After identifying pivots, we construct a semi-supervised discrepancy that nudges each non-pivot's predicted rating distribution toward similar pivots. The construction has two parts [18], [19]: a differentiable representation of a user's predicted rating distribution and a pivot-aware, soft-neighborhood calibration penalty.
Here $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the sigmoid function and temperature parameter $\tau>0$ sets smoothness.

Each coordinate $f^{(k)}$ approximates the fraction of predictions below threshold $t_{k}$. Thus $\mathbf{g}_{u}$ aggregates a series of smooth empirical cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of predicted ratings. This representation not only captures more distribution information beyond simple and non-differentiable mean and variance, but also reduces noise given sparse labeled data, and, specifically, is end-to-end optimizable with respect to model parameter, $\theta$, gradient backpropagation. The aggregated function $\mathbf{g}_{u}\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{u}\right)$ has a good theoretical property, as shown in Lemma 2 in [18], [19], that $\mathbf{g}_{u}\left(\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{u}\right) \rightarrow F_{u}(k)= \operatorname{Pr}\left[\hat{r}_{u, i} \leq \frac{k}{K} \cdot r_{\text {max }}+\frac{K-k}{K} \cdot r_{\text {min }}\right], k \in\{1, \ldots, K\}$, i.e. converges to the emperical cumulative distribution distribution of the predicted ratings when $\tau \rightarrow \infty$, serving as the basis for later rating distribution calibration to other users'.

This design (1) emphasizes closer pivots without committing to a single reference, (2) uses distribution features computed from predictions over all items (not only labeled ones), and (3) remains fully differentiable for joint training.

sec.label: C.
sec.title: Overall Optimization Algorithm
sec.p: where $\mu>0$ is a hyperparameter that balances prediction and calibration.

The whole training algorithm is shown in 1. The joint objective ensures that (1) supervision on $\mathcal{O}$ fits observed labels, while (2) $\mathcal{L}_{\text {semi-cal }}$ transfers distributional structure from pivots to non-pivots, reducing user-level selection bias without relying on a fully specified propensity or imputation model. Because $\mathbf{g}_{u}$ pools predictions across all items, calibration is informed by the model's global belief about each user, not just the few labeled pairs-precisely the regime where unbiased labels are scarce and uneven.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Experiment
sec.p: We evaluate SemiSL-RDC on two widely used benchmarks (Yahoo! R3 and Coat) under the standard MNAR-with-few-unbiased-labels protocol for debiasing in recommendation. Our empirical study is designed to answer:

alg-item.p: Input: Set of user-item features $X$; All user-item pairs
matrix $\mathcal{D}$; Observed user-item pairs matrix $\mathcal{O}$; Hyperpa-
Initialize the prediction model $f_{\theta}$ by pre-training it on the
for number of training iterations do
For each user, calculate the pivot-user probability $\pi_{u}$
For each non-pivot user $u \in \mathcal{P}_{-}$, calculate its distri-
bution difference between each pivot user $v \in \mathcal{P}_{+}$,
Calculate calibration loss $\mathcal{L}_{\text {semi-cal }}$, and overall loss

list-item.p: RQ1: Effectiveness. Does SemiSL-RDC improve accuracy when per-user unbiased labels are scarce and uneven?

RQ2: Competitiveness. How does it compare with strong debiasing families (propensity reweighting, doubly-robust learning, multi-task variants, knowledge-distillation/meta-learning that use unbiased data, residual/balancing methods, and prior distributioncalibration)?
RQ3: Robustness across regimes. Are gains consistent across datasets with markedly different unbiasedcoverage patterns (near-zero vs. small fixed per-user coverage)?
RQ4: Semi-supervised pivoting. Does semi-supervised pivot identification provide additional benefits over prior self-supervised calibration?

sec.label: A.
sec.title: Datasets

list-item.p: Yahoo! R3 dataset contains 15,400 users $\times 1,000$ items with 311,704 self-selected (MNAR) ratings and a small unbiased slice of 54,000 ratings collected by asking 5,400 users to rate 10 randomly chosen items. Overall observation density $\approx 2.37 \%$ (97.63 % unlabeled). Peruser unbiased (MAR) coverage ranges $0-1 \%$ ($0 / 1000$ for users not in the MAR cohort; 10/1000 for MAR users). These figures are standard in the literature and reflect how scarce and uneven unbiased supervision is on Yahoo! R3. [14], [22], [52]–[54]

Coat dataset contains 290 users $\times 300$ items with 24 MNAR + $\mathbf{1 6}$ MAR ratings per user (6,960 MNAR and 4,640 MAR total). Overall observation density $\approx 13.33 \%$ (86.67 % unlabeled). Per-user unbiased (MAR) coverage

caption.title: PERFORMANCE IN TERMS OF AUC, NDCG@5, AND RECALL @ 5 on the UNBIASED DATASET OF COAT AND YAHOO! R3. THE BEST RESULTS ARE BOLDED, AND THE BEST BASELINE RESULTS ARE UNDERLINED.

sec.p: is fixed at $\mathbf{5. 3 3} \%(16 / 300)$. This design is commonly used to emulate controlled unbiased slices for evaluation. [14], [55], [56]

These statistics echo our problem setting: the unbiased pool is small and uneven (Yahoo!), or small but uniform (Coat), reinforcing the need to (i) leverage predictions over all items when forming distribution features and (ii) transfer guidance from MAR-like pivots to non-pivots.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Baselines
sec.p:
We compare our methods to the following debiasing methods in recommendation system:
•
Base model: the Matrix Factorization (MF) model [57] is trained on biased data, uniform data and both of them respectively, denoted as MF (biased), MF (uniform) and MF (combine). •
Propensity-based estimators: IPS [22], [58], DR [24], [25], and their robust/residual variants (RD-IPS/DR, BRD-IPS/DR [59]). •
Multi-task learning approaches that leverage auxiliary tasks or counterfactual towers: ESMM [60], MultiIPS/DR [61], ESCM2-IPS/DR [62]. •
Methods using both biased and unbiased data: CausE (knowledge distillation with uniform data), KD-Label, KD-Feature, AutoDebias, DR-LTD (bi-level learning-todebias). •
Methods using both biased and unbiased data: CausE (a sample-based knowledge distillation approach) [63], KDLabel, KD-Feature [64], AutoDebias [65]. •
Calibration-based: RDC (distribution matching via pivot users) [18], [19].

sec.label: C.
sec.title: Implementation Details
sec.p: All models are implemented in PyTorch and optimized with Adam. We tune learning rate in $\left\{10^{-4}, 5 \times 10^{-4}, 10^{-3}, 5 \times 10^{-3}, 10^{-2}\right\}$ and weight decay in $\left\{0,10^{-6}, 10^{-5}, 10^{-4}, 10^{-3}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-1}\right\}$. For SemiSL-RDC we tune soft-CDF bins $K \in\{16,32,64\}$, temperature $\tau \in\{5,10,20\}$, neighborhood temperature $\gamma \in\left\{5 \times 10^{-3}, 10^{-2}, 5 \times 10^{-2}\right\}$, calibration weight $\lambda_{\text {cal }} \in\{0.1,0.5,1\}$, and pivot-score weights $\left(\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}, \alpha_{3}\right) \in \{(0.4,0.4,0.2),(0.5,0.3,0.2),(0.5,0.25,0.25)\}$. Evaluation uses AUC, NDCG@5, Recall@5.

sec.label: D.
sec.title: Performance analysis
sec.p: Table I reports AUC, NDCG@5, and Recall@5 on two benchmarks, Yahoo! R3 and Coat. Across both datasets and all three metrics, most debiasing methods outperform the MF baseline, underscoring the necessity of addressing selection effects. Relative to IPS/DR and their variants, SemiSL-RDC attains comparable or better results without introducing additional propensity or imputation models. Methods that exploit both biased and unbiased data (e.g., distillation/meta-learning)

are strong overall, but SemiSL-RDC remains competitive despite using a lightweight calibration loss. Improvements hold on both Yahoo! (highly uneven per-user unbiased coverage) and Coat (small but fixed per-user coverage), suggesting that distributional matching against MAR-like anchors is beneficial under heterogeneous coverage regimes. SemiSL-RDC targets the complementary regime where per-user unbiased coverage is extremely sparse and heterogeneous, since semi-supervised pivoting stabilizes anchor discovery by blending labeled MAE with unlabeled entropy and coverage. The resulting pivots yield more reliable calibration targets and consistent gains over self-supervised RDC.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This work introduced SemiSL-RDC, a semi-supervised rating-distribution calibration framework for mitigating selection bias in recommendation. We first identify pivot users-those whose feedback appears closer to MAR-via a multi-criteria score that integrates per-user MAE (on labeled pairs), normalized rating-distribution entropy, and labeledcoverage. We then align each non-pivot user's predicted rating distribution to similar pivots using a differentiable soft-CDF representation and a pivot-aware calibration loss that augments the base cross-entropy objective. By constructing distribution features from all predictions (labeled and unlabeled) and using a deterministic pivot set, SemiSL-RDC offers a stable, model-agnostic route to reduce bias when unbiased supervision is scarce and uneven. Empirical results on two realworld datasets indicate consistent improvements over a span of state-of-art debiasing families, including propensity-based, doubly-robust, knowledge distillation, and prior distributioncalibration baselines, highlighting the practical value of semisupervised pivot discovery plus distribution matching.

conf-article.back:

Li, C. Zheng, P. Wu, K. Kuang, Y. Liu, and P.
